name: Libya
experiences: Tripoli medina lanes and Assaraya Alhamra
Ghadames old town's covered Berber streets
Saharan dune runs by 4x4 outside Ghadames
avoid: Strong safety advisory: Libya remains politically unstable and many governments advise against travel; only consider a trip with a vetted local operator, fixed hotel transfers, and enough slack for abrupt closures or permit changes.
Do not self-drive between cities or move after dark: checkpoints, militia activity, and changing local control can turn a routine coast or desert leg into a same-day cancellation.

Most major sites, especially Ghadames and the big Roman ruins, work best with cash, a guide, and printed passport copies because card payments, posted tariffs, and opening hours can all be unreliable.
stopoverNote: Tripoli is the only sensible logistics base for most foreign visitors: land there, keep the coast-ruin days as daylight returns, and treat Ghadames as a separate block reached by a pre-arranged domestic flight or a fully organized long road transfer.

cities.name: Tripoli
cities.notes: Libya's gateway city for the medina, Martyrs' Square, the Red Castle/Assaraya Alhamra, and all the operator, SIM, cash, and permit logistics before heading out.

cities.name: Leptis Magna
cities.notes: UNESCO-listed Roman port city east of Tripoli with the Severan forum, Hadrianic baths, basilica, amphitheatre, and one of North Africa's most complete archaeological spreads.

cities.name: Sabratha
cities.notes: Mediterranean UNESCO site west of Tripoli famed for its three-storey Roman theatre, forum temples, basilica remains, and compact site museum.

cities.name: Ghadames
cities.notes: Remote oasis near the Algeria-Tunisia frontier with a UNESCO old town of roof-linked white houses, palm gardens, and desert 4x4 excursions to high dunes.

itinerary.sim: Libyana Mobile Phone and Almadar are the two practical prepaid options in Tripoli; budget roughly ₹850–₹2,550 for a tourist top-up with enough data for WhatsApp, maps, and operator calls, then expect patchy service once you are out near Ghadames.

activities.name: Red Castle / Assaraya Alhamra Museum entry
activities.tip: Recent traveler reports place foreigner entry around 10–15 LYD, but closures and security checks can change access on short notice.

activities.name: Hotel-arranged airport transfer or secure city taxi hops
Gurgi Mosque exterior and Karamanli quarter walk
Medina shopping and cash run before the coast or desert
Guide, permit, and daylight-transfer briefing for Sabratha, Leptis Magna, or Ghadames

days.theme: Roman Theatre, Forum Temples & a West-Coast Daylight Run

activities.name: Sabratha archaeological site entry
activities.tip: Recent traveler reports put the main site ticket at about 10 LYD per person.

activities.name: Roman theatre and stage-building circuit
Adjacent Sabratha museum
activities.tip: When the small museum is open, reports suggest a separate fee of roughly 5 LYD.

activities.name: Private taxi or driver from Tripoli on the coastal road

days.theme: Severan Forum, Basilica & North Africa's Grandest Roman Day

activities.name: Leptis Magna archaeological site foreigner entry
activities.tip: Recent operator quotes cluster around 50–100 LYD for foreigners, although many tours bundle the ticket and gate procedures into the day rate.

activities.name: Septimius Severus arch, basilica, and forum circuit
Leptis Magna museum or small-site add-on
activities.tip: Museum access is often included; if separated, expect only a small extra cash charge.

activities.name: Taxi or pre-arranged driver from Tripoli or Al Khums

days.theme: UNESCO Old Town, Covered Alleys & Mint Tea in the Desert Oasis

activities.name: Licensed guide for Ghadames old town
activities.tip: The old town itself is generally treated as free to enter, but foreign visitors are typically expected to hire an authorized local guide costing about 50–100 LYD per group.

activities.name: Courtyard tea stop in the old town
Traditional home lunch in the old quarter
activities.tip: Recent local operator guidance puts a hosted old-town meal at around 50–80 LYD.

activities.name: Ras al Gule Roman-fortress viewpoint drive

days.theme: Palm Gardens, Saharan Dunes & 4x4 Desert Running

activities.name: 4x4 dunes excursion outside Ghadames
Taguella sand-bread or desert picnic stop
Palm-grove walk and sunset on the desert edge
Driver transfer to Ghadames airport or onward road checkpoint

connections.method: Private taxi or hotel-arranged driver on the coastal road; keep it strictly daylight because the westbound leg is security-sensitive after dark.
Most travelers route back through Tripoli, then continue east by the same driver or a fresh private taxi toward Al Khums and Leptis Magna.
Return to Tripoli for a domestic flight when schedules operate; otherwise use a fully pre-arranged long-haul 4x4 or driver convoy to the oasis.
Domestic flight is the cleanest exit when operating; road returns are long and should only run with current local security clearance and a vetted driver.

extras: Best months are October to March: the coast is pleasant, Roman sites are easier to walk, and Ghadames desert days are far more comfortable than the furnace-like late spring and summer.

Treat Libya as a high-risk destination rather than a casual Mediterranean add-on. Political instability, fragmented authority, and sudden local closures mean you should re-check advisories before departure and before every intercity leg.
Red Castle, Leptis Magna, and Sabratha fees can still be paid in cash on site, but foreigner procedures are sometimes handled by a guide first, so carry enough Libyan dinars and do not rely on cards.
Domestic flights inside Libya can appear, vanish, or retime with little notice; build in a Tripoli buffer night before any international departure.
Ghadames sightseeing usually pairs an old-town guide with tourist-police coordination and a 4x4 for the dunes, so it works best as an organized overnight rather than an improvised same-day dash.
